state_doh_release:
Alaska Department of Health Authorizes Expanded Syringe Services Program Coverage
The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under Alaska public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms.

Arkansas Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 291 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Arkansas treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

Arizona Department of Health Services Pilots Drug Checking and Fentanyl Test Strip Distribution
Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 21 authorized harm reduction sites across Arizona. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Arizona statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage.

California Department of Public Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Connecticut Department of Public Health Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Connecticut law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

DC Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in the District of Columbia that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 74 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

Delaware Division of Public Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
Delaware health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 11 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Dover area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for cocaine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Delaware Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Mecklenburg, Cuyahoga counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $3,800,000 Bureau of Justice Assistance grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division. --- Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $13,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 447 additional recovery housing beds across 9 provider organizations in Iowa. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards.

Illinois Department of Public Health Releases Provisional Suspected-Overdose Surveillance Update
Provisional data from the Illinois syndromic surveillance system indicate a slight increase of approximately 8 percent in suspected nonfatal overdose emergency department encounters compared with the same period one year earlier. Surveillance counts are preliminary and subject to revision as additional records are reported and toxicology completes. The Department continues to publish these data monthly through its DOSE-SYS dashboard alongside national CDC data.

Following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray, Kansas Department of Health and Environment confirms that Narcan is now available without a prescription at participating retail pharmacies statewide, including major chains. The Department continues to operate 74 community distribution sites and supplies free naloxone to approximately 24 community-based organizations through state and federal opioid response funding. Residents may also request free naloxone by mail through the state harm-reduction program. The Department reminds the public that naloxone reverses opioid overdose only and is safe to administer; bystanders cannot harm a person who does not have opioids in their system.

Massachusetts Department of Public Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Massachusetts medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 21 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- Massachusetts Department of Public Health Opens Naloxone Vending Machines and Harm-Reduction Kiosks
Naloxone vending machines providing free Narcan nasal spray, fentanyl test strips, and overdose education materials are now operational at 12 locations across Massachusetts, including Springfield, Boston, Worcester. The machines operate 24 hours a day and require no identification, prescription, or fee. Use of the machines is anonymous and protected under state public health authority. The Department will continue to expand kiosk placement in partnership with libraries, transit hubs, and community organizations.

Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Missouri continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 52 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

Pennsylvania Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 5 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Arkansas Department of Health Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Arkansas continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 46 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications. --- Arkansas Department of Health Announces Expanded Over-the-Counter Naloxone Availability

Washington State Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Washington medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 28 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Alaska Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Alaska medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 27 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- Alaska Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 57 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- South Carolina Department of Public Health Announces Expanded Over-the-Counter Naloxone Availability
Following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray, South Carolina Department of Public Health confirms that Narcan is now available without a prescription at participating retail pharmacies statewide, including major chains. The Department continues to operate 109 community distribution sites and supplies free naloxone to approximately 47 community-based organizations through state and federal opioid response funding. Residents may also request free naloxone by mail through the state harm-reduction program. The Department reminds the public that naloxone reverses opioid overdose only and is safe to administer; bystanders cannot harm a person who does not have opioids in their system. --- South Carolina Department of Public Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
South carolina health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 22 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Greenville area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for xylazine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the South Carolina Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

South Dakota Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 91 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the South Dakota treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

Tennessee Department of Health Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Tennessee law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed. --- Tennessee Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder

Texas Department of State Health Services Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Texas continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 54 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

Virginia Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Virginia that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 68 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Virginia Department of Health Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Marion, Fulton counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $3,400,000 SAMHSA grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

Vermont Department of Health Opens Naloxone Vending Machines and Harm-Reduction Kiosks
Naloxone vending machines providing free Narcan nasal spray, fentanyl test strips, and overdose education materials are now operational at 17 locations across Vermont, including South Burlington, Rutland, Burlington. The machines operate 24 hours a day and require no identification, prescription, or fee. Use of the machines is anonymous and protected under state public health authority. The Department will continue to expand kiosk placement in partnership with libraries, transit hubs, and community organizations.

Washington State Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation. --- Washington State Department of Health Pilots Drug Checking and Fentanyl Test Strip Distribution
Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 18 authorized harm reduction sites across Washington. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Washington statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage.

Toxicology reports from Alaska medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 35 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Arizona Department of Health Services Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Oklahoma State Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Oklahoma medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Toxicology reports from Michigan medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 23 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Oregon Health Authority Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Arkansas Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

DC Health Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from the District of Columbia continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 40 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

Toxicology reports from Alabama medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 35 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 3 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $3,700,000 Bureau of Justice Assistance grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

Idaho Department of Health and Welfare Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 191 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Idaho treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

Indiana Department of Health Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $8,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 213 additional recovery housing beds across 17 provider organizations in Indiana. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards. --- Indiana Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Indiana medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 53 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Kansas Department of Health and Environment Pilots Drug Checking and Fentanyl Test Strip Distribution
Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 19 authorized harm reduction sites across Kansas. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Kansas statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage. --- Kansas Department of Health and Environment Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Kansas continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 51 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications. --- Kansas Department of Health and Environment Recognizes International Overdose Awareness Day
Joining communities worldwide, Kansas Department of Health and Environment recognizes International Overdose Awareness Day. In Kansas, suspected nonfatal overdose emergency department encounters totaled approximately 720 in the most recent month for which CDC DOSE-SYS data are available. The Department reminds the public that drug overdose is preventable: free naloxone, fentanyl test strips where lawful, and connection to medication for opioid use disorder are available through approximately 29 county health departments and partner harm reduction organizations. The Department encourages residents to carry naloxone and to learn the signs of opioid overdose.

Under Maryland law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

Maine Department of Health and Human Services Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Maine that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 53 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Maine Department of Health and Human Services Opens Naloxone Vending Machines and Harm-Reduction Kiosks

As students return for the school year, the Department is distributing fentanyl awareness materials to school nurses, counselors, and parent organizations across Missouri. The materials emphasize that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source — including pills shared by classmates or purchased through social media — may contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. School-based naloxone training is available to school nurses and athletic trainers, and several districts have stocked naloxone in school health offices. --- Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services Announces Expanded Over-the-Counter Naloxone Availability
Following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray, Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services confirms that Narcan is now available without a prescription at participating retail pharmacies statewide, including major chains. The Department continues to operate 149 community distribution sites and supplies free naloxone to approximately 65 community-based organizations through state and federal opioid response funding. Residents may also request free naloxone by mail through the state harm-reduction program. The Department reminds the public that naloxone reverses opioid overdose only and is safe to administer; bystanders cannot harm a person who does not have opioids in their system.

Mississippi State Department of Health Recognizes International Overdose Awareness Day
Joining communities worldwide, Mississippi State Department of Health recognizes International Overdose Awareness Day. The Department reminds the public that drug overdose is preventable: free naloxone, fentanyl test strips where lawful, and connection to medication for opioid use disorder are available through approximately 21 county health departments and partner harm reduction organizations. The Department encourages residents to carry naloxone and to learn the signs of opioid overdose. --- Mississippi State Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Florida Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Florida medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 46 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Alaska Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 7 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Toxicology reports from New Mexico medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 40 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Mississippi statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage.

Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Montana law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
North carolina health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 11 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Durham area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for xylazine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the North Carolina Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Nebraska continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 49 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

New Jersey Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 253 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the New Jersey treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

New Mexico Department of Health Releases Provisional Suspected-Overdose Surveillance Update
Provisional data from the New Mexico syndromic surveillance system indicate a slight increase of approximately 16 percent in suspected nonfatal overdose emergency department encounters compared with the same period one year earlier. Surveillance counts are preliminary and subject to revision as additional records are reported and toxicology completes. The Department continues to publish these data monthly through its DOSE-SYS dashboard alongside national CDC data. --- New Mexico Department of Health Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Philadelphia, Hamilton, Davidson counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $3,600,000 CDC Overdose Data to Action grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

New York State Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in New York that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 48 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

Arkansas Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Arkansas medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 63 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment Authorizes Expanded Syringe Services Program Coverage
The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under Colorado public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms. --- Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 3 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 4 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from North Carolina medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 56 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 5 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation. --- Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Iowa medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 27 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Authorizes Expanded Syringe Services Program Coverage
The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under Iowa public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms.

Maine Department of Health and Human Services Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Maine law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $4,200,000 HRSA grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment Pilots Drug Checking and Fentanyl Test Strip Distribution
Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 20 authorized harm reduction sites across Colorado. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Colorado statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage.

Connecticut Department of Public Health Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $11,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 241 additional recovery housing beds across 14 provider organizations in Connecticut. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards. --- Connecticut Department of Public Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
Connecticut health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 13 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Bridgeport area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for methamphetamine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Connecticut Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

Delaware Division of Public Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 284 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Delaware treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

Iowa Department of Health and Human Services Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Iowa law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

The Department announces the addition of approximately 110 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Idaho treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization. --- Idaho Department of Health and Welfare Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Idaho continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 54 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications. --- Idaho Department of Health and Welfare Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Idaho that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 45 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

Louisiana Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 2 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Oklahoma State Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 2 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Arkansas Department of Health Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under Arkansas law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for benzodiazepine analogues. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Rhode Island Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help. --- Rhode Island Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 269 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Rhode Island treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

South Dakota Department of Health Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from South Dakota continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 39 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications. --- South Dakota Department of Health Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Fulton, Kanawha, Cook counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $1,800,000 CDC Overdose Data to Action grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

Alaska Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 5 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

Arkansas Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Arkansas that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 50 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Arkansas Department of Health Announces Expanded Over-the-Counter Naloxone Availability
Following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray, Arkansas Department of Health confirms that Narcan is now available without a prescription at participating retail pharmacies statewide, including major chains. The Department continues to operate 186 community distribution sites and supplies free naloxone to approximately 39 community-based organizations through state and federal opioid response funding. Residents may also request free naloxone by mail through the state harm-reduction program. The Department reminds the public that naloxone reverses opioid overdose only and is safe to administer; bystanders cannot harm a person who does not have opioids in their system.

Toxicology reports from Hawaii medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 29 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- Hawaii Department of Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses

New York State Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids

Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 55 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. California Department of Public Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply

Toxicology reports from Delaware medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 33 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Hawaii Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 2 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation. --- Hawaii Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl

Arkansas Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 7 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Massachusetts Department of Public Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Massachusetts medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. DC Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from the District of Columbia medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- DC Health Pilots Drug Checking and Fentanyl Test Strip Distribution
Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 29 authorized harm reduction sites across the District of Columbia. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under the District of Columbia statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage.

Wyoming Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 5 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation. --- Wyoming Department of Health Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Wyoming continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 28 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

West Virginia Department of Health and Human Resources Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 7 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation.

The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under Wisconsin public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms. --- Wisconsin Department of Health Services Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Wisconsin continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 26 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

West Virginia Department of Health and Human Resources Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under West Virginia law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed. --- West Virginia Department of Health and Human Resources Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
West virginia health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 7 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Charleston area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for methamphetamine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the West Virginia Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

Wyoming Department of Health Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $6,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 304 additional recovery housing beds across 21 provider organizations in Wyoming. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards.
